metadata.career_relevance: Zerodha's trading platform handles 3 million concurrent users during the Diwali trading window. Without performance testing, they'd discover the bottleneck at 9:15am when markets open — too late. Gatling simulates 3M virtual users, finds the breaking point in pre-production, and identifies the bottleneck (database connection pool exhausted at 50K users). After this topic, you can write performance tests that catch scalability issues before they reach production.

hook.real_world_problem:
Zerodha Diwali 2023 without performance testing:

```
09:15am: Markets open. 2.8 million users log in simultaneously.
09:15:30: Database connection pool: EXHAUSTED (max 200 connections)
09:15:45: All API calls: connection timeout
09:16:00: Kite app: 502 Bad Gateway for all users
09:16:30: Social media: #ZerodhaDown trending
09:17:00: Estimated loss: ₹50 lakh/minute (missed trades)

With Gatling performance test (run in staging, week before):
Simulated 3M users → connection pool exhausted at 50K
Fix: pool size 200 → 2000 + read replicas for portfolio queries
Diwali actual: 2.8M users → all good → no outage
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Gatling load test for a payment API with response time assertions, and a k6 stress test script.

concept_explanation.test_types:
**Performance test types:**

```
LOAD TEST: normal expected load
  Simulate: 1000 users over 30 minutes
  Question: 'Can we handle normal traffic?'
  Pass criteria: p99 < 500ms, 0 errors
  When: every feature release

STRESS TEST: beyond normal load until failure
  Simulate: ramp up 100 → 1000 → 5000 → 10000 users
  Question: 'Where does it break? How does it fail?'
  Pass criteria: fails gracefully (503, not data corruption)
  When: quarterly, before big events (Diwali, IPO launch)

SOAK TEST: normal load for extended time
  Simulate: 500 users for 8 hours
  Question: 'Does it degrade over time? Memory leaks?'
  Pass criteria: response time stable over 8 hours
  When: pre-launch, detecting memory leaks

SPIKE TEST: sudden massive spike
  Simulate: 100 → 5000 users in 10 seconds
  Question: 'Can it recover from sudden traffic spike?'
  When: before flash sales, breaking news events
```

visual_aid.description: Three side-by-side graphs. Graph 1 (Load test): steady ramp to 1000 users, flat for 10min, stable p99 response time. Graph 2 (Stress test): staircase ramp 100→1000→5000, error rate spikes and p99 explodes at 5000 — breaking point identified. Graph 3 (Soak test): 500 users flat for 8 hours, response time gradually increasing (memory leak pattern). Below: Gatling HTML report screenshot with request count, mean, p95, p99, max, error rate columns.
visual_aid.alt_text: Load, Stress, and Soak test patterns with Gatling report metrics

worked_example.scenario: Load test for Razorpay payment creation API.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Gatling scenario: POST /api/payments with HDFC gateway
2. Inject: 10 at once + ramp 100 over 30s
3. Assertions: p99 < 500ms, success rate > 99%
4. @SpringBootTest: server starts, basic load test runs

step_by_step_solution.details: p50 (median): 50% of requests faster than this. p95: 95% of requests faster. p99: 99% faster. MAX: slowest request. Use p99 for SLA (not average — averages hide tail latency). Zerodha: 'p99 < 500ms' means 99% of trade submissions complete in under 500ms. The worst 1% can be slow, but 99% must be fast.

worked_example.expected_output:
================================================================================
---- Global Information ----
> request count: 7250 (OK=7214 KO=36)
> min response time: 12 (OK=12 KO=502)
> mean response time: 187 (OK=185 KO=512)
> p95 response time: 342
> p99 response time: 478
> max response time: 891
---- Errors ----
> status.find.is(200), but actually found 503   (36)
================================================================================
> Global: percentage of successful requests is above 99%  KO (99.5 > 99.0) PASS
> Global: 99th percentile of response time is below 500    478 < 500 PASS
things_to_notice: percentile3 in Gatling DSL = p99 (not p3!). Naming convention: percentile1=p50, percentile2=p75, percentile3=p99. Always check the Gatling docs for the exact mapping.
KO requests (36): Gatling counted 36 failures. In stress tests, some failures are expected. The assertion 99% success means up to 1% failure is acceptable.
Baseline first: before running the load test, measure a single-user baseline (p99 should be 50-100ms). If baseline is already 400ms, adding load will definitely fail the 500ms threshold.

Gatling vs JMeter: Gatling uses Akka (non-blocking, can simulate thousands of users per machine). JMeter uses threads (one thread per user — 1000 users = 1000 threads, memory-intensive). For cloud-scale testing: Gatling scales better.

industry_examples.use_case: Every year before the Diwali trading window, Zerodha runs 3M-user Gatling stress tests against staging. Tests reveal: DB connection pool limit, Redis cache miss rate under load, Kafka consumer lag at peak. Engineers fix each bottleneck before the actual holiday. Result: zero outages during the highest-volume trading day of the year.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you ensure your system can handle 10x load?' → Performance testing. Gatling load tests: simulate expected load, assert p99 < SLA. Stress tests: ramp to breaking point, ensure graceful degradation. Identify bottlenecks: DB connections, thread pools, memory. Fix: connection pool sizing, caching, horizontal scaling. Run in CI: every release, catch regressions. Baseline: measure single-user metrics before load testing.
applications.real_world_workflow: Performance testing workflow: (1) establish single-user baseline, (2) run load test at expected traffic, (3) run stress test to find breaking point, (4) fix bottlenecks (connection pools, caching, scaling), (5) re-run to verify fixes, (6) run soak test for memory leak detection, (7) CI gate: merge blocked if p99 > SLA.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Running load tests against production ('it's the only real environment'). Load tests hammer the database with test data, can cause real outages, and corrupt production data.
common_gaps.remediation: Load test environment: staging (same spec as production, isolated database, real network topology). Never run load tests against production databases. Use production-like data (anonymized snapshot). Monitor production separately.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Load test URL points to production endpoint; test data appears in production monitoring dashboards

key_takeaways: Load test: normal traffic, validates SLA under expected load
Stress test: beyond normal, finds breaking point
Soak test: extended duration, detects memory leaks and degradation
p99 for SLA: not average (averages hide tail latency)
Gatling: Akka-based, non-blocking, high concurrency per machine
k6: JavaScript, developer-friendly, great CI integration
Baseline first: single-user metrics before load testing
CI gate: block merge if p99 regresses

self_check_questions.question: A Zerodha trade API has p50=100ms and p99=800ms. Is this acceptable for a 500ms SLA?
self_check_questions.answer: No — the 500ms SLA is violated. The p99 = 800ms means 1% of users experience 800ms+ response times. With 1 million daily trades, 1% = 10,000 trades hitting slow response. The p50=100ms is misleading — the median looks fine but tail latency is terrible. Root cause: likely a slow code path triggered only in certain conditions (complex order type, specific stock, cache miss). Fix: identify what's special about the 1% slow cases. Check: database query without index for edge case, cold cache on first request per user, synchronous external API call on the slow path. The SLA must use p99 or p95 — never just p50.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 25 topic: Chaos Engineering — deliberately inject failures (latency, exceptions, crashes) into your system to verify it handles them gracefully.
